title: Kim Kelly Is My Friend

jokes.text: Donut chaos: cookies/donuts dropped on the floor, and the response is 'Now they're dirty, so just eat them.'
jokes.setup: Students are squabbling over sprinkle donuts meant for French class; someone drops them on the hallway floor.
jokes.punchline: Rather than throwing them away, the logic is 'now they're dirty, so just eat them' — a resigned, pragmatic non-solution that is funnier than outrage.
jokes.explanation: A low-key opener that establishes the show's naturalistic tone. The 'just eat them' line is recognizably human — the kind of defeated logic real teenagers use — but it's not crafted enough to be memorable. Functional scene-setter.

jokes.text: Daniel, on Karen's post-breakup fury: 'Well, if she's looking for new customers, I'm up for a test drive.'
jokes.setup: Karen is visibly furious after being dumped; someone explains she's 'on the warpath.'
jokes.punchline: Daniel immediately reframes her rage as sexual availability, using a car-dealership metaphor — crude but completely in character.
jokes.explanation: Works because it's pure Daniel — opportunistic, tone-deaf, delivered with total confidence. The 'test drive' metaphor is economical and character-specific. The immediate 'Shut up, Daniel' response is the punctuation the joke needs. Mid-tier but well-executed character comedy.

jokes.text: Perry flexes and accidentally punches Sam in the solar plexus while showing off his workout gains.
jokes.setup: Perry is bragging about working out and flexing dramatically for Karen.
jokes.punchline: He doesn't see Sam standing there and hits him, then cheerfully says 'Sorry, little guy. Didn't see ya. You all right, pal?' — completely unbothered.
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy setup — oblivious jock, invisible small kid. The 'little guy / pal' condescension while Sam is gasping for air is the real joke. The secondary beat of Sam being informed what happened to him ('solar plexus') while still unable to breathe extends it nicely.

jokes.text: Neal proposes: 'I propose after school we make some acid, pour it on the sidewalk, and watch it kill bugs.'
jokes.setup: Neal announces his brother sent him an amazing chemistry set, implying something exciting is coming.
jokes.punchline: The grand proposal is to use dangerous acid... to kill bugs on the sidewalk for entertainment.
jokes.explanation: This works on two levels: the misdirect (chemistry set implies something impressive) and the character-specific bathos (these are 14-year-olds who think bug-killing is a worthy scientific pursuit). Neal's formal 'I propose' language against the pettiness of the goal is the joke's engine. Also a setup for the later chemistry set payoff.

jokes.text: Sam accidentally bangs Karen's locker, then nervously explains he got hit in the solar plexus as his excuse.
jokes.setup: Sam is already rattled from being hit; he stumbles into the wrong locker while Karen is in a foul mood.
jokes.punchline: His explanation — 'I just got hit in the solar plexus, so I'm a little out of it' — is both true and the most pathetically useless thing he could possibly say to an angry girl.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the earlier punch pays off here — Sam's situation compounds. His honesty-as-incompetence is recognizably adolescent. Karen's complete indifference ('I don't wanna hear your life story') is a well-timed straight-man response.

jokes.text: Sam, post-solar-plexus hit, accidentally bangs on Karen Scarfoli's locker and is immediately threatened
jokes.setup: Sam is dazed from the solar plexus hit and mistakenly tries to open the wrong locker
jokes.punchline: It belongs to Karen Scarfoli — the most dangerous girl in school — who immediately erupts on him
jokes.explanation: Good structural comedy — the earlier injury has immediate consequences that land Sam in worse trouble. The escalation from 'dazed kid' to 'facing school menace' is earned. The callback elevates it above a standalone bit.

jokes.text: Karen's friends mock Sam for being 14: 'You can't be 14, midget. You haven't even hit puberty.' Then one checks his armpits.
jokes.setup: Sam tries to defend himself by stating his age as proof he belongs there.
jokes.punchline: Rather than accepting this, the girls escalate to physically inspecting his armpits for hair, treating him like livestock being evaluated.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from verbal mockery to physical inspection is what sells this. It's the kind of specific, humiliating thing that could only happen in a high school hallway. The armpit inspection is so boldly weird and degrading that it produces genuine discomfort-laughter. Pure Freaks and Geeks cringe.

jokes.text: Karen marks Sam's locker with graffiti 'so you'll always know which one's yours and which one's mine.'
jokes.setup: Sam has just been humiliated publicly; Karen takes a marker to his locker.
jokes.punchline: She frames this act of vandalism/bullying as a practical favor — helping Sam remember — delivered with total straight-faced sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the reframing — cruelty presented as helpfulness. Karen's deadpan 'OK, geek?' at the end treats Sam like she's done him a service. The gap between what she's doing and how she describes it is the comedic engine.

jokes.text: Bill, after Sam returns from the locker incident, asks with genuine innocent curiosity: 'You don't have hair in your pits?'
jokes.setup: Sam has just survived a public humiliation involving armpit inspection.
jokes.punchline: Bill's first and only reaction is to sincerely ask about the armpit hair — no sympathy, no outrage, just genuine geek curiosity about the most embarrassing detail.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best small moments. Bill's delivery cuts straight to the embarrassing core, bypassing all social awareness. It's pure Bill — no filter, no malice, just relentless literal-mindedness. The economy is exceptional: five words that perfectly capture both the character and the absurdity of Sam's situation. It's the kind of line that only Bill Haverchuck could deliver.

jokes.text: Daniel asks about Karen's breakup: 'Is she, like, a prude? Did she not, you know... go the distance?' Everyone laughs at 'distance.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is fishing for information about Karen's sexual availability, trying to be smooth.
jokes.punchline: His euphemism 'go the distance' is so oddly clinical and sports-adjacent that everyone laughs at the word choice itself.
jokes.explanation: Daniel's inability to just ask a direct question, combined with a hilariously inappropriate sports metaphor, is character-specific comedy. The in-universe laugh at 'distance?' validates the joke without over-explaining it.

jokes.text: Kim describes the Karen/Ricky situation: 'She's not a prude. She's a slut, obviously.' Beat. 'She's a friend of yours.' Kim: 'Obviously.'
jokes.setup: The group is gossiping about Karen. Kim has insider knowledge.
jokes.punchline: Kim's flat, matter-of-fact 'obviously' to both statements — her friend being a slut and that being self-evident — delivered without a hint of judgment or irony.
jokes.explanation: The double 'obviously' is the joke's structure — Kim's breezy acceptance of both facts with equal nonchalance. It reveals her worldview efficiently. The economy is excellent: she says so much about herself with one repeated word.

jokes.text: After the group gossips extensively, Kim says: 'God, people are so gossipy at this school. It's, like, get a life, you know?'
jokes.setup: Kim has just been actively participating in and driving the gossip about Karen's sex life.
jokes.punchline: Having led the gossip session, she suddenly positions herself as above it all — with zero self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: A solid irony joke — Kim's total lack of self-awareness is the punchline. It's character-specific (this hypocrisy is very Kim) and the timing of the reversal is clean. Not transcendent, but a well-earned laugh.

jokes.text: Lindsay tries to sit in a different spot; someone says 'That's Gary Ross's seat.' Lindsay: 'Oh. But he's sick.' 'He has impetigo.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is fleeing Kim and tries to find a different seat; the seat is taken by an absent student.
jokes.punchline: The specific diagnosis of 'impetigo' — a grotesque skin condition — delivered flatly as if it answers all further questions.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'impetigo' is the joke — a mundanely grotesque detail that slightly derails the scene. Low-key funny but not especially crafted. Background texture more than a proper joke.

jokes.text: Nick defends Lindsay hanging out, then undercuts himself: 'She shouldn't ruin everything for everybody just because she's always on her period.'
jokes.setup: Nick is trying to diplomatically advocate for Lindsay being included in the group.
jokes.punchline: His attempt at sensitivity collapses with 'just because she's always on her period' — stated as plain biological fact, not as an insult.
jokes.explanation: Nick's good intentions colliding with his complete social ineptitude is funny and character-specific. The line isn't malicious — Nick genuinely thinks he's being reasonable — which is what makes it land.

jokes.text: Neal's Twilight Zone riff: 'A defaced locker... a humiliated freshman... and a busty succubus from hell — perhaps we've left McKinley High and entered the chilling corridors of the Twilight Zone.'
jokes.setup: Sam returns humiliated from his Karen encounter; Neal has observed the whole situation.
jokes.punchline: Neal's pompous Rod Serling impression, culminating in 'busty succubus from hell,' delivered with complete unearned confidence.
jokes.explanation: 'Busty succubus from hell' is a great phrase — it reveals Neal's simultaneously nerdy and weirdly adult vocabulary. The Twilight Zone framing is a little long but the payoff is specific to him. Sam's flat 'You're not funny' response is the correct punctuation.

jokes.text: Bill: 'I can't believe she inspected your pits. You know, I thought she was gonna make sweet love to ya.'
jokes.setup: Sam is still processing the humiliation of the armpit inspection.
jokes.punchline: Bill reinterprets the physical invasion of the armpit check as potentially sexual — again with zero emotional filter — and delivers 'make sweet love to ya' in the most deadpan possible register.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. 'Make sweet love to ya' is a phrase so incongruous with Bill's age, delivery, and the situation that it produces a genuine laugh. It's the third beat in a running armpit gag and escalates perfectly. Only Bill Haverchuck could say this with that specific deadpan sincerity. The phrase itself is quotable and the callback structure is tight.

jokes.text: Harold's rule vs. Harold's other rule: 'My dad has a rule: you don't fight girls.' / 'My dad has a rule: you don't piss your pants at school.'
jokes.setup: Sam says his dad has a rule about not fighting girls, using it to explain his helplessness.
jokes.punchline: Neal immediately counters with his own dad's rule — a rule so much more embarrassing and specific that it implicitly mocks Sam's situation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect parallel structure — the joke relies entirely on the rhythm of 'My dad has a rule...' being repeated with a devastating substitution. 'You don't piss your pants at school' implies this has happened, makes us wonder about Neal's family, and obliterates Sam's attempt at dignity. Economical, well-structured, and the follow-up 'Hey, I didn't piss my pants!' confirms Sam's complete loss of control of the situation.

jokes.text: Karen: 'I'm just gonna have to mark that locker again. This time... in geek blood.'
jokes.setup: Karen is threatening to re-graffiti the locker after Sam washed it off.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from marker graffiti to 'geek blood' — phrased with theatrical villainy — is absurdly over-the-top for a locker dispute.
jokes.explanation: 'In geek blood' is a genuinely funny escalation — the melodrama is completely disproportionate. It paints Karen as a person who genuinely lives in a more dramatic register than everyone around her.

jokes.text: Bill, after Karen leaves: 'I, uh, I hope she meant your geek blood.'
jokes.setup: Karen has threatened to write in 'geek blood.' The three geeks are all present.
jokes.punchline: Bill's quietly selfish clarification — he hopes it's Sam's blood specifically, not a general geek-blood situation — delivered with complete deadpan.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. Bill's complete lack of solidarity — prioritizing his own safety over Sam's, stated with total casual deadpan — is quintessential Bill Haverchuck. The economy is exceptional: one short sentence that's simultaneously cowardly, logical, and completely oblivious to social norms. It's specific to this character in this moment and instantly quotable. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: Kim on phone with her mother, screaming: 'Would you quit yelling at me, you psychopath?!' then hangs up and says, 'Well, as usual, my mother is being a total shrew.'
jokes.setup: Kim has been screaming on the phone; Lindsay witnesses it.
jokes.punchline: Kim's immediate pivot to composed understatement — 'total shrew' — after seconds of unhinged screaming reframes her own behavior as the reasonable party.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Kim's screaming and her calm self-assessment is the joke. It also establishes the Kelly family dysfunction efficiently. The 'as usual' implies this is routine, which deepens both the comedy and the pathos.

jokes.text: Nick, watching Kim's phone meltdown: 'You know, sometimes relationships can be a drag. Especially if you're goin' out with her.'
jokes.setup: Kim has been screaming 'you psychopath' at her own mother on the phone.
jokes.punchline: Nick's understated observation, immediately followed by a sheepish apology, captures his character perfectly — a stoner's relaxed commentary on obvious chaos.
jokes.explanation: Nick's gentle stoner wisdom applied to an extremely abnormal situation. 'Especially if you're goin' out with her' is the right length for the punchline. His apologetic 'Sorry' afterward completes the character portrait.

jokes.text: Student about Kim: 'I heard about what she does in the yearbook darkroom.' Then, when asked to clarify: 'She... fornicates it.'
jokes.setup: Someone has heard rumors about Kim's behavior in the darkroom and hints broadly at it.
jokes.punchline: 'She fornicates it' — using 'fornicates' as an intransitive verb with a direct object, which is grammatically broken and hilariously euphemistic at the same time.
jokes.explanation: 'She fornicates it' is a memorable and specific joke — the word choice is technically wrong (you can't 'fornicate' a darkroom) and that grammatical incorrectness is the point. It's the awkward vocabulary of someone who knows the word but not the usage. Quotable and specific.

jokes.text: Millie responds to Kim's alleged darkroom activities: 'I just think it's sad.' Lindsay: 'We can't all be Girl Scouts, Millie.'
jokes.setup: Millie is being pious about Kim's reputation.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's dismissal using 'Girl Scouts' as a synonym for moral perfection — aimed specifically at Millie, who probably is one.
jokes.explanation: Works because of character specificity — 'Girl Scouts' aimed at Millie is perfectly calibrated, and it shows Lindsay already defending Kim before they're even friends. The line is economical and character-revealing.

jokes.text: Geeks discover 'Pygmy' has been written on Sam's locker. Bill explains: 'It's a dwarf from Australia.'
jokes.setup: Someone has added to the locker humiliation with a new insult: 'Pygmy.'
jokes.punchline: Bill defines 'pygmy' as 'a dwarf from Australia' — confidently, helpfully, and completely wrong (pygmies are from Africa/Asia). His certainty about incorrect information is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Bill being confidently, helpfully wrong is perfect character comedy. The specific wrong answer ('Australia') is funnier than just not knowing — he has the wrong information and is sharing it with authority. This is exactly how Bill Haverchuck works.

jokes.text: Vice principal catches Sam by his graffitied locker and demands an essay: '500 words — Why Vandalism — Why the Property of School Should Be Respected and Why We Should Respect It.'
jokes.setup: Sam is the victim of Karen's vandalism, not the perpetrator.
jokes.punchline: Despite Sam's protest ('It wasn't me' — 'Well, yeah, but—'), the principal assigns punishment to the victim. The essay title itself is redundant and incoherent ('Why Vandalism — Why the Property...').
jokes.explanation: The institutional injustice landing on the victim is a classic school comedy setup. The essay topic itself — which changes wording midway through without the principal noticing — is a nice detail. Sam's partial admission ('It wasn't me. Well, yeah, but—') seals his fate perfectly.

jokes.text: Sam is eating aggressively before dinner: 'I'm loading up on carbs.' Mom: 'But we're having carbs for dinner.' Sam: 'You want me to stay a midget? Is that what you want?'
jokes.setup: Sam's mother catches him eating before dinner.
jokes.punchline: Sam frames his snacking as a medical necessity and accuses his mother of wanting him to remain a midget — a wild escalation from 'don't spoil your dinner.'
jokes.explanation: Sam has internalized the day's mockery and redirected it at his mother as if height-anxiety is now his primary medical concern. 'Is that what you want?' is an accusation so disproportionate to the situation it's excellent. The callback to 'midget' from earlier in the episode adds resonance.

jokes.text: Sam on Kim Kelly: 'Kim Kelly's a psycho. She and Karen Scarfoli? They're violent. They run around the school being evil.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay's mother asks what Kim is like.
jokes.punchline: 'They run around the school being evil' — stated as plain factual description, with complete seriousness, as if 'being evil' is a recognized extracurricular activity.
jokes.explanation: 'Being evil' as a matter-of-fact activity is the joke — Sam's completely sincere, unexaggerated description sounds like a dramatic overstatement but isn't, which is the Freaks and Geeks sweet spot.

jokes.text: Sam, explaining why Lindsay shouldn't befriend Kim: 'Because she and her friends are mean to me. They wrote Geek on my locker.' Lindsay: 'Well, maybe you are a geek.'
jokes.setup: Sam is making an earnest case for why Kim is bad news, using his own humiliation as evidence.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's 'well, maybe you are a geek' — delivered as a sincere point before she catches herself.
jokes.explanation: This is a great sibling moment. Lindsay's accidental honesty — validating Sam's tormentors before realizing what she's said — is both funny and slightly cruel. The extended awkward beat where she tries to walk it back ('I'm just kidding... Oh come on, Sam, like I meant that') compounds the damage. Earns its score through character specificity and the gap between intention and impact.

jokes.text: Kim pre-briefs Lindsay on her parents: 'My aunt Kathy was so rad. She lived in L.A. She was on Kojak. She doinked Ryan O'Neal once at a party.' Beat. 'Yeah. Well, she's dead. She O.D.'d on coke.'
jokes.setup: Kim is telling Lindsay about her awesome aunt who gave her the Gremlin.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from glamorous details (Kojak, Ryan O'Neal) to 'she's dead, she O.D.'d on coke' — delivered with the same casual tone as the glamour details.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best extended character moment. Kim rattles off her aunt's cool credentials (TV show, celebrity sex) with real pride, and then drops 'she's dead / she O.D.'d on coke' without changing tone — because in Kim's world, these are all just facts about her aunt. The word 'doinked' is perfect. The tonal flatness of the tragic reveal is what makes it land. It simultaneously generates a laugh and builds Kim's backstory as a character whose life has just always been this way.

jokes.text: Kim, immediately after the aunt death story: 'Roll up my window. I don't want my car to get stolen.'
jokes.setup: Kim has just shared a deeply personal story about her dead aunt.
jokes.punchline: Abrupt pivot to mundane car safety, as if the emotional conversation is simply over.
jokes.explanation: A good punctuation beat — the abruptness of the topic switch communicates volumes about Kim's emotional style. Brief and economical.

jokes.text: Kim, as a side note while briefing Lindsay on the cover story: 'Oh, and by the way, I spend the night at your house every Friday night, and last weekend, we went up to your vacation house in Benton Harbor and went water-skiing.'
jokes.setup: Kim has been asking Lindsay to follow her lead with her parents.
jokes.punchline: Kim casually reveals the elaborate lie she's already told — a vacation home, water-skiing, weekly sleepovers — that Lindsay is expected to maintain, having been told about it 20 seconds before arriving.
jokes.explanation: The casualness with which Kim drops this impossible demand — 'oh, and by the way' — as if it's a minor logistical note, is great comedy. Lindsay's 'We...what?' is the correct response. Sets up the dinner disaster that follows.

jokes.text: Kim forgot to bring soda. Her mom: 'Yeah? Well, I forgot to make you dinner. How do you like that?'
jokes.setup: Kim's mother greets them and immediately points out Kim forgot the soda.
jokes.punchline: Mom's nuclear response — threatening to withhold dinner as a proportional response to forgetting soda — establishes the Kelly household's escalation dynamic immediately.
jokes.explanation: A perfect establishing joke for the Kelly family. The proportionality is completely off — soda for dinner — and the 'How do you like that?' is the kind of passive-aggressive escalation that defines this household. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Kim's mom: 'She's hanging on for dear life.' Kim: 'B.S. I get good grades.' Mom: 'I hate to blow your mind, sister, but Ds are not good. Ds are bad.'
jokes.setup: Kim insists she gets good grades.
jokes.punchline: Mom's deadpan explanation that 'Ds are not good, Ds are bad' — delivered as if Kim needs this clarified — is condescending and funny.
jokes.explanation: 'I hate to blow your mind, sister, but Ds are not good. Ds are bad.' is a well-constructed line — the 'I hate to blow your mind, sister' setup implies something complex is coming, and then the information is as simple as possible. Good economy.

jokes.text: Kim explains her D: 'I told you my history teacher was a pervert. I wasn't about to go to his house after school to get tutored.'
jokes.setup: Kim is defending her bad grade to her mother.
jokes.punchline: Kim's explanation involves a predatory teacher — stated matter-of-factly, as a reasonable excuse — which is both a dark revelation and utterly plausible given everything we know about Kim's world.
jokes.explanation: This line works on several levels at once: it's a defense of her grade, a character revelation (Kim navigates a world with real predators), and it's funny precisely because it's probably true. The quotes around 'tutored' she'd presumably imply in delivery complete the picture. It briefly tips the show's dramedy balance toward dark territory.

jokes.text: Chip is asleep/snoring during the dinner conversation. 'Can it, Kim. You're gonna wake up Chip.' [Snoring continues.]
jokes.setup: A tense family conversation is happening at the table.
jokes.punchline: A seemingly random family member named Chip is just... asleep, presumably at the table, and 'waking Chip' is apparently the priority.
jokes.explanation: The visual (a person named Chip is just asleep in the background of this chaotic dinner) is a perfect background joke. The show doesn't explain Chip or make it a big deal — he's just there, snoring, and 'Can it, Kim, you're gonna wake up Chip' is a hilariously misaligned priority in the middle of family conflict.

jokes.text: Stepfather asking about the Weir family's wealth: 'You got a vacation house up there in Benton Harbor... your family must be very wealthy.' Lindsay: 'My dad always says a farmer never reveals how many cows he's got.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is trying to maintain Kim's cover story about the Weirs being wealthy.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay invents a folksy proverb on the spot — 'a farmer never reveals how many cows he's got' — as if this is a known saying that explains why she can't discuss wealth.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Lindsay, under pressure, improvises a completely made-up proverb with perfect fake-rural gravitas. 'A farmer never reveals how many cows he's got' sounds plausible enough that the stepfather laughs at it rather than questioning it. It's original, specific, quotable, and reveals Lindsay's intelligence even while she's lying. The delivery — 'My dad always says' — commits completely to the bit. Justifies the high score.

jokes.text: Stepfather, upon hearing Lindsay's dad owns A-1 Sporting Goods: 'Really? Can he get us a discount?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay mentions her dad owns a sporting goods store after the family has been probing for signs of wealth.
jokes.punchline: The immediate pivot from 'your family must be very wealthy' to asking for a store discount — the most petty, practical, small-minded possible response.
jokes.explanation: Character-establishing comedy. The stepfather's immediate 'Can he get us a discount?' deflates his earlier wealth-envy into mundane opportunism. Brief and well-timed.

jokes.text: Lindsay tries to explain why the Weirs' house isn't as fancy as advertised: 'We're remodeling.' Kim's stepfather: 'Oh, and take your time with that. I love relievin' myself behind a shower curtain.'
jokes.setup: The Kelly house visibly lacks a proper bathroom; Lindsay tries to explain the Weir house's missing amenities.
jokes.punchline: The stepfather reveals — while insulting his own house — that they don't have a proper bathroom, only a shower curtain, and he hates it.
jokes.explanation: 'I love relievin' myself behind a shower curtain' is specific, unexpected, and slightly TMI — exactly right for the character. The sarcasm is clear without being telegraphed.

jokes.text: Kim's stepfather on the bathroom: 'We're remodeling.' / 'Oh, and take your time with that. I love relievin' myself behind a shower curtain.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay (or someone) notices the bathroom is unfinished
jokes.punchline: The stepfather sarcastically celebrates having to urinate behind a shower curtain — a detail so specific it implies it's been going on a while
jokes.explanation: 'I love relievin' myself behind a shower curtain' — the specificity of 'relievin' myself' (vs. just 'using the bathroom') adds to the comedy. It paints a very specific, unglamorous picture. The Kelly household's dysfunction is established through comic details like this.

jokes.text: Kim's younger sibling immediately follows the bathroom revelation with: 'Smells like fart.'
jokes.setup: The stepfather has made a self-deprecating remark about the bathroom.
jokes.punchline: A child's candid addition — 'smells like fart' — confirms and escalates the bathroom situation with maximum bluntness.
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfect delivery, total commitment from a child character. It's low-craft but effective — the economy score is exceptional because nothing could be more economical. A kid saying 'smells like fart' during a tense dinner scene breaks every tension instantly.

jokes.text: Back in the Weir kitchen: Neal refuses to hold the test tube for chemistry experiments, citing a magazine story about a kid who got acid on him and 'now he's goin' to Disneyland for his last hoorah.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are doing chemistry experiments; Neal is asked to hold a test tube.
jokes.punchline: Neal refuses and explains his reasoning via a story about a child so badly burned by acid that his dying wish is Disneyland — 'his last hoorah' — delivered as a perfectly reasonable precaution.
jokes.explanation: 'Goin' to Disneyland for his last hoorah' is a great phrase — it implies a child is dying from a chemistry set accident and someone arranged a Make-A-Wish Disneyland trip. Neal delivers this as a cautionary tale with complete sincerity. Dark, specific, and very Neal.

jokes.text: Neal's chemistry set logic: 'I'm not gonna get my fingers melted off just so you can get a kick.' / Bill's response: 'Why? So you can pick your rumpus?'
jokes.setup: Neal is refusing to hold a test tube for Bill, who needs the experiment done
jokes.punchline: Bill's counter — 'so you can pick your rumpus?' — using 'rumpus' as a childish euphemism for buttocks, which is both funny for its childishness and because it's an oddly specific threat about what Neal would do with working fingers
jokes.explanation: 'Rumpus' is a Bill word — it's exactly the kind of thing Bill would say with total sincerity. The weird formality of 'pick your rumpus' is funnier than any cruder alternative would be.

jokes.text: Bill asks how many words Sam has. Sam says 33. Bill: 'It's so unfair! I mean, if I have to write an essay, then Karen Scarfoli should have to write a big, fat book.'
jokes.setup: Sam is struggling with his 500-word essay after only 33 words.
jokes.punchline: Bill's proportional outrage — Karen should write a 'big, fat book' — and his instant solidarity with Sam's punishment despite Bill not having to write anything.
jokes.explanation: 'A big, fat book' is the right escalation from 'essay.' Bill's genuine indignation on Sam's behalf is sweet and funny — he's outraged even though it doesn't affect him at all.

jokes.text: Bill: 'I think she's a sex fiend. You should move your locker. What if she comes to school really horny one day?'
jokes.setup: The geeks are discussing Karen Scarfoli's behavior.
jokes.punchline: Bill's practical advice — move lockers to avoid potential sexual aggression from an angry girl — delivered as a genuine safety concern.
jokes.explanation: Bill's combination of absurd logic and genuine helpfulness is core to his character. 'What if she comes to school really horny one day?' is presented as a legitimate threat assessment. The non-sequitur practicality is very Bill.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Girls don't get horny.' Bill: 'They don't?' Neal: 'Only guys get horny.' Bill: 'Maybe Karen Scarfoli's a guy.'
jokes.setup: Neal is confidently explaining that girls don't experience sexual desire.
jokes.punchline: Bill accepts Neal's (wrong) premise and applies it logically: if girls don't get horny, and Karen does, therefore Karen is a guy. Delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Bill's logical syllogism — accepting Neal's wrong premise and reaching the only conclusion available from it — is genuinely well-structured comedy. Neal's confident wrongness followed by Bill's equally confident extension of it is peak geek chemistry. The character integration is exceptional: only these two characters would have this exact conversation with this exact earnestness.

jokes.text: Neal calls Sam 'Bilbo Baggins' again; Sam retorts: 'Yeah? Well, at least I'm not a pygmy.'
jokes.setup: Neal is continuing to mock Sam about his locker humiliation.
jokes.punchline: Sam reaches for the only insult available to him — the 'pygmy' label Neal coined earlier — turning it back on its inventor.
jokes.explanation: The callback works but doesn't escalate. Sam using the only available weapon against Neal is recognizably what a cornered 14-year-old would do. Functional.

jokes.text: Lindsay is trapped by Kim's mother's questions about the vacation house. 'What street is it on?' Lindsay: 'Benton Harbor... Street.'
jokes.setup: Kim's mom has been pressing Lindsay with increasingly specific questions about the fictional vacation house; Lindsay has been winging it.
jokes.punchline: 'Benton Harbor Street' — Lindsay gives the name of the town as the name of the street, the most catastrophically unimaginative lie possible, with a desperate pause between the two words.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. The sustained cringe of the interrogation builds pressure expertly, and 'Benton Harbor... Street' is the perfect release — it's the worst possible answer, arrived at through sheer desperation, with a pause that shows Lindsay realizing as she says it that it's wrong. The buildup, the pause, the street name as town name — it's a masterclass in cringe comedy structure. Justifies 8+ clearly.

jokes.text: The Kelly dinner explodes: Kim's mom: 'You are a lying brat! I knew it!' Kim: 'I'm not lying! You guys just can't believe that I can make a friend who's rich and smart!' Mom: 'She's not smart!' Kim: 'No, she is! She's really smart! I swear to God!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay's cover is completely blown; the dinner has dissolved into chaos.
jokes.punchline: In the middle of the fight, Kim pivots to desperately defending Lindsay's intelligence — 'She's really smart! I swear to God!' — as if that's the important issue.
jokes.explanation: Kim's misdirected loyalty — defending Lindsay's intelligence while her own entire life is blowing up — is both funny and touching. It's an accidental reveal of genuine feeling amidst chaos.

jokes.text: The Kelly family dinner erupts: Kim's mom explodes about the car, Kim yells 'You're not my father, fat ass!' / stepfather: 'Damn right, I'm not!'
jokes.setup: The lie is exposed; the family dynamic collapses into a full screaming match
jokes.punchline: 'Damn right, I'm not!' — the stepfather confirming he's not her father as if it's a winning argument, which accidentally validates Kim's insult
jokes.explanation: The 'Damn right, I'm not!' button is the joke within the chaos. He's agreeing with her while trying to disagree — the outrage accidentally confirms the exact point she was making. Good escalation; this is when Lindsay is just trying to survive the dinner.

jokes.text: Kim's stepfather: 'Your aunt Kathy spent $1,000 of my money snorting it up her nose, so as far as I'm concerned, that car is mine!'
jokes.setup: Kim is fighting to keep her dead aunt's car.
jokes.punchline: The stepfather reveals the dead aunt owed him $1,000 from a cocaine debt — weaponizing her death in the car argument.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Aunt Kathy's death takes a darker turn — the stepfather's grievance is financial, not emotional, and the '$1,000 of my money snorting it up her nose' line recontextualizes the aunt's death as an economic dispute. Funny and bleak in equal measure.

jokes.text: Neal confronts Sam: 'I'm sorry to break it to you, but you're the geek.' Sam: 'I may be some things, but I'm not a geek.' Neal: 'Hey, you guys, let's go make some acid and pour it on the sidewalk. Won't that be fun?' [Beat.] 'You're 14 years old and you still play with chemistry sets?'
jokes.setup: Neal is calling Sam the geek of the group.
jokes.punchline: Neal immediately proposes the exact thing that makes him a geek — making acid to kill bugs — and then Sam turns it back on him by pointing out he plays with chemistry sets.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully structured callback. Neal calls Sam a geek, then immediately proves his own geekiness by reprising the exact chemistry set proposal from the opening. Sam's 'you're 14 and you play with chemistry sets' retort uses Neal's own logic against him. The irony is tight and the callback structure is elegant. Justifies the high score.

jokes.text: Sam denies playing with Tonka trucks. Neal: 'You want to go on the record with that statement?' Sam: 'I gave them away, like, years ago.' Neal: [dramatically] 'May I direct the jury's attention to Exhibit A?'
jokes.setup: Sam denies the Tonka truck accusation.
jokes.punchline: Neal reveals physical evidence ('Exhibit A') and frames it as a courtroom proceeding — the theatrical legal performance is completely disproportionate to a fight about toys.
jokes.explanation: Neal's courtroom performance is peak Neal — pompous, theatrical, and completely committed to winning a juvenile argument by any means necessary. 'May I direct the jury's attention to Exhibit A' over a Tonka truck is excellent character comedy.

jokes.text: The geeks ask Bill to adjudicate who's the bigger geek: Bill's response: 'I don't know. I mean, what's geek mean anyway? It's just a word.' Pushed to choose: 'I guess... Sam.'
jokes.setup: Sam and Neal both appeal to Bill as the tie-breaker in the geek argument.
jokes.punchline: Bill first attempts philosophy ('what's geek mean anyway'), then when forced to choose, picks Sam immediately — having just made the case that labels don't matter.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best character jokes. Bill's philosophical deflection ('it's just a word') followed by instantly specific betrayal of Sam is perfect — the structure is a trap. Bill refuses to engage with labels in principle, then has no problem applying them in practice. The pause before 'I guess... Sam' is crucial: it implies actual deliberation, which makes the outcome funnier. This is exactly the kind of Bill Haverchuck moment that defines the character. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Neal consoling Sam about being the designated geek: 'Hey, you shouldn't feel so ashamed. There are a lot of famous geeks in history — Einstein, Gilligan, Gomer Pyle.'
jokes.setup: Sam is upset about being voted the biggest geek.
jokes.punchline: Neal's list of 'famous geeks' smoothly includes Einstein alongside fictional TV characters Gilligan and Gomer Pyle, as if they belong in the same pantheon of historical achievers.
jokes.explanation: The Einstein-Gilligan-Gomer Pyle list is a great joke. Neal transitions from the greatest scientist of the 20th century directly to fictional TV bumblers with no change in register, as if these are equally valid historical exemplars of geekdom. 'Gomer Pyle' as the endpoint of the list is perfect — it's the most bathetic possible landing. Very Neal, very quotable.

jokes.text: The chemistry set spills during the fight; Bill shouts 'Cover your flesh! Cover your flesh!'
jokes.setup: The Tonka truck fight escalates physically and knocks over Neal's chemistry set.
jokes.punchline: Bill's panicked instruction 'Cover your flesh! Cover your flesh!' — delivered with total alarm, paying off Neal's earlier acid-on-flesh story.
jokes.explanation: 'Cover your flesh! Cover your flesh!' is a great payoff to the Disneyland acid story. The phrase itself is funny — 'your flesh' is a strange way to refer to your skin — and the repetition is perfect panic comedy. A callback that earns its place.

jokes.text: Kim finds Daniel at the basketball court with another girl and screams: 'You are dead! You are so dead! Do you hear me? You are dead! Both of you are dead!'
jokes.setup: Kim has driven to find Daniel and found him with another girl.
jokes.punchline: Kim's threat escalates from 'dead' to 'so dead' to confirming both parties are dead — the repetition and intensification of the same word with diminishing new information.
jokes.explanation: Kim's fury is funny in its purity — it's all the same word, just intensified. Not a technically sophisticated joke, but completely in character and the delivery would sell it.

jokes.text: Nick's understated reaction to Kim nearly running them over and then screaming 'You are dead': 'She's pissed.'
jokes.setup: Kim has driven at them, screamed, and peeled away.
jokes.punchline: Nick's two-word analysis after an extremely obvious demonstration of Kim's anger.
jokes.explanation: 'She's pissed' after someone nearly runs you over and screams 'you are dead' multiple times is a beautifully understated reaction. Nick's stoner deadpan reduces Kim's operatic fury to two words. Economy is nearly perfect — you can't say less.

jokes.text: Kim, crying about Daniel: 'Daniel is a normal guy, you know? He'll screw anything that moves. And that's why I've got to stay alert. I have to be a bitch, you know?'
jokes.setup: Kim is tearfully explaining her relationship with Daniel to Lindsay.
jokes.punchline: Kim's self-aware 'I have to be a bitch' as a defensive strategy — she's not unaware of her behavior, she's decided it's necessary, and delivers it as a perfectly logical conclusion.
jokes.explanation: This is one of Kim's defining character moments. 'He'll screw anything that moves' as characterization of her own boyfriend, combined with the logical conclusion 'that's why I have to stay alert / I have to be a bitch' — Kim is not self-deceived. She understands her situation clearly and has made a strategic choice. It's both very funny and genuinely sympathetic. 'You know?' at the end is Kim checking for understanding, which makes it more human. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Kim: 'Oh, my life sucks!' Lindsay: 'No, it doesn't.' Kim: 'My parents hate me! I don't have any friends! You're, like, my only friend, Lindsay, and you're — you're a total loser.' Beat. 'No offense.'
jokes.setup: Kim is venting about how terrible her life is and trying to find comfort.
jokes.punchline: 'You're a total loser' dropped in the middle of an emotional declaration that Lindsay is her only friend — then 'no offense' as an afterthought, as if that fixes it.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The structure is nearly perfect: Kim is making a genuine, emotionally vulnerable statement about Lindsay being her only friend, and the line about Lindsay being a 'total loser' arrives mid-sentence as if it just slipped out — because for Kim, it's just true. The 'no offense' is the masterpiece final beat — it acknowledges the insult while not taking it back. The joke works as comedy, as character revelation, and as a weirdly touching moment. The character integration is exceptional: this could only be Kim Kelly. Justifies 9+.

jokes.text: Sam is eating aggressively at dinner; Mom: 'Oh, he usually never eats, and just look at him.' Dad: 'He could use a little meat on his bones.' Mom: 'I'm just worried he might have worms.' Dad: 'Sam, do you think you might have worms?'
jokes.setup: Sam is eating ravenously after the day's stresses.
jokes.punchline: His mother's medical concern escalates from 'eating a lot' to 'might have worms' — and his father simply asks Sam directly if he has worms, at the dinner table.
jokes.explanation: Harold asking Sam directly 'do you think you might have worms?' at the dinner table is a perfect Weir parent moment. It's blunt, embarrassing, and completely earnest. The escalation from 'eating a lot' to 'worms' as a medical hypothesis is excellent parent comedy.

jokes.text: Kim, crying at the Weir dinner table; Sam burps loudly. Kim keeps crying. Sam burps even louder. Kim's father: 'Damn.' Mom: 'What is it, Sam? Is he going to blow up?' Dad: 'That's a perfectly good piece of veal wasted. I think he has a parasite.'
jokes.setup: Kim is in tears at the Weir dinner table; the family is trying to be comforting.
jokes.punchline: Sam's escalating burps punctuate the emotional moment, and Harold's response to the burping is 'that's a perfectly good piece of veal wasted / I think he has a parasite' — completely redirecting from Kim's emotional crisis to Sam's digestive situation.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Kim's genuine emotional distress and Sam's body betraying him with increasingly dramatic burps is excellent physical comedy. Harold's pivot to worrying about the veal rather than Kim or Sam is perfect Harold — he's practical even in emotional moments. 'I think he has a parasite' is a callback that lands harder than the first time. The scene earns its score through layering: physical comedy, cringe, character comedy, all simultaneously.

jokes.text: Kim, watching TV with Harold: 'I hate cop shows. Cops are such pigs. My brother got ambushed by cops once. They beat him on the head with their billy clubs.' Harold: 'What was your brother doing?' Kim: 'Nothing. He was just drunk, minding his own business.'
jokes.setup: Kim is making conversation with Lindsay's dad while watching TV.
jokes.punchline: 'Nothing. He was just drunk, minding his own business.' — Kim's completely sincere defense of her brother's innocence, where 'being drunk' is presented as an entirely legitimate and unimpeachable activity.
jokes.explanation: 'He was just drunk, minding his own business' is an excellent line — 'just drunk' as a modifier that implies baseline innocence is perfect. Kim has no idea this is a self-undermining statement. The character integration is excellent; this kind of oblivious self-revelation is core to Kim's appeal.

jokes.text: Kim's mother calls the Weirs: she tells Jean that Lindsay and Kim 'stay out all night and sleep with boys' and that she 'shouldn't believe a word' Lindsay says.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has barely escaped the Kelly house; she thinks the night's chaos is over.
jokes.punchline: Kim's mother retaliates by calling Lindsay's mother and delivering a maximalist character assassination — which is partially true, partially not, and entirely disproportionate.
jokes.explanation: The Kelly mother's revenge call extending the chaos into the Weir household is a satisfying escalation. The specific accusations ('sleep with boys') are funny because they're what Mrs. Kelly apparently believes about her own daughter, projected onto Lindsay.

jokes.text: Harold, clearly uncomfortable with Nick making moves on Lindsay: 'Hey, what do you say we guys go down to the beach and shoot some clams?'
jokes.setup: Nick has been cozying up to Lindsay; Harold is watching this with barely concealed discomfort.
jokes.punchline: Harold's completely random, inexplicable 'shoot some clams' — a non-existent activity — as a way to separate Nick from Lindsay.
jokes.explanation: 'Shoot some clams' is a masterpiece of protective-dad incompetence. Harold needs to separate Nick and Lindsay, invents an activity on the spot, and lands on something that doesn't even exist as a thing people do. The suggestion is so random and wrong that it's immediately funny. Harold's desperation is perfectly captured in those three words. Quotable and specific to this character.

jokes.text: Kim continues: 'Now he sleeps all day on the couch, and he never parties. I think he's got brain damage.'
jokes.setup: Kim is describing what happened to her brother after the police beating.
jokes.punchline: Kim's indicators of brain damage are 'sleeps all day on the couch' and 'never parties' — the latter being the most concerning symptom from her perspective.
jokes.explanation: 'He never parties. I think he's got brain damage.' — 'never parties' as the clinical symptom that confirms brain damage to Kim is a perfect character joke. From Kim's worldview, not partying is the most alarming behavioral change possible. Earned, specific, very quotable.

jokes.text: Nick shows up at the Weir door as Kim's messenger: 'Daniel's waiting out at the car, and he wants to talk to Kim.' Kim: 'What are you, his chore boy? Why don't you go tell your master that I don't ever want to talk to him again?'
jokes.setup: Nick has been sent to fetch Kim on Daniel's behalf.
jokes.punchline: 'What are you, his chore boy?' — Kim reduces Nick's errand to servitude and calls Daniel his 'master,' which is more accurate than anyone wants to admit.
jokes.explanation: 'Chore boy' and 'master' are specific enough to be funny. Nick's social position in the friend group — always the one sent on errands — makes this land harder.

jokes.text: Harold, after 'so you can all just bite me' from Kim: 'If she's planning on sleeping over, she can just forget about it.'
jokes.setup: Kim has just told everyone in the Weir house to 'bite me' and stormed off.
jokes.punchline: Harold's measured, quiet, firm response — 'she can just forget about it' — is the bureaucratic parental response to a guest telling everyone to bite them.
jokes.explanation: Harold's understated authority in the middle of emotional chaos is a running character note throughout the episode. 'She can just forget about it' after 'bite me' is perfectly calibrated — he's not angry, he's just drawing a reasonable line.

jokes.text: Kim promises Sam revenge on Karen: 'I'm just going to break her arms. And after I break her arms, I'm going to take a wrench and pull out her teeth one by one. And then I'm going to take a match and set her hair on fire.' Sam: 'Well, don't hurt her too bad.'
jokes.setup: Kim is telling Sam she'll deal with Karen for him.
jokes.punchline: Kim's revenge plan escalates from arm-breaking to dental extraction to arson, all described with methodical calm — and Sam's response is a gentle 'well, don't hurt her too bad.'
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is clean and funny — arms, then teeth-with-a-wrench, then hair-fire is a satisfying comedic sequence that gets increasingly specific and gruesome. Sam's measured 'don't hurt her too bad' is the perfect straight-man response — he's not horrified by the plan, just calibrating the severity. The juxtaposition of Kim's cheerful specificity and Sam's polite moderation is excellent.

jokes.text: Daniel sneaks in through a window/door and scares Jean: 'AAH! HAROLD!' / Daniel: 'Lady, I'm not going to hurt you. I'm just looking for my girlfriend.'
jokes.setup: Daniel comes into the Weir house looking for Kim — apparently through an unexpected entrance
jokes.punchline: His reassurance — 'I'm not going to hurt you' — is the exact thing a threatening person would say, and 'I'm just looking for my girlfriend' makes everything weirder
jokes.explanation: Daniel entering and immediately alarming the mother is a good physical beat. His reassurance is classically counter-productive. Good suburban-home-invaded-by-freaks comedy.

jokes.text: Harold, on Daniel: 'Jeez, kid, you ever hear of a doorbell? People do have heart attacks, you know.' / Daniel: 'Oh, yeah. Sorry, pops.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has startled Jean by entering unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: Harold's complaint is about doorbell etiquette — the most Harold Weir response possible to a leather-jacketed freak invading his home. Daniel's 'Sorry, pops' is both charming and oblivious.
jokes.explanation: Harold reduces Daniel's dramatic entrance to a doorbell complaint, which is perfectly Harold. 'People do have heart attacks, you know' is a dad-threat. 'Sorry, pops' from Daniel — who clearly is not sorry and has never called anyone 'pops' — is charming and slightly manipulative. Their brief rapport is a great character contrast.

jokes.text: Nick meets Lindsay's parents for the first time: 'I just want you to know it's, like, such a pleasure to meet you guys finally. Uh, Lindsay turned out great! She's... so great. Congratulations.'
jokes.setup: Nick appears unexpectedly at the Weir house; Lindsay introduces him to her parents.
jokes.punchline: Nick congratulates Jean and Harold on raising Lindsay — as if raising a child is an achievement to be applauded like winning a race — delivered with genuine stoned sincerity.
jokes.explanation: 'Congratulations' for raising your daughter is one of the episode's best lines. Nick's stoned earnestness transforms what should be a normal introduction into something completely bizarre. 'She's... so great. Congratulations.' implies he has evaluated Lindsay and found the results impressive, and the parents deserve credit for the product. The pause before 'so great' and the finality of 'Congratulations' are what make it exceptional. Justifies the high score.

jokes.text: Nick, after complimenting the Weirs: 'Hey, so, um, listen, do you guys mind if I have this Fruit Roll-Up?'
jokes.setup: Nick has just delivered an unexpectedly moving tribute to the Weirs as parents.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from heartfelt compliment to casually asking if he can eat their Fruit Roll-Up — as if these are equivalent social transactions.
jokes.explanation: The Fruit Roll-Up callback is perfect. Nick's munchies — implied throughout — finally find their target in the Weir pantry. The casualness of the ask, immediately after 'Congratulations,' is a great tonal drop. Harold's 'Please, go ahead. Enjoy.' is the correct defeated-parent response.

jokes.text: Jean: 'I think they've made up. Let's go check on them.' / Lindsay: 'Mom, no.' / Jean: 'I think we deserve to see a happy ending, don't you, Harold?'
jokes.setup: Kim and Daniel have apparently reconciled in Lindsay's room
jokes.punchline: Jean wants to go witness the make-up scene — a completely inappropriate impulse framed as something they've 'earned' after all the chaos
jokes.explanation: Jean's obliviousness is the joke — she genuinely thinks going to watch teenagers make up (in Lindsay's room, privately) is appropriate. 'I think we deserve to see a happy ending' is delivered with complete sincerity. The 'don't you, Harold?' co-opting of the dad is a great family-dynamic beat.

jokes.text: Daniel is doing post-reconciliation small talk with Harold: 'I think you got a great store. There's a lot of great stuff in there. I bought a basketball in there a couple of years ago. It still works and everything.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is trying to make peace with Lindsay's dad after the scene he caused.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's review of the Weir sporting goods store — culminating in 'It still works and everything' as the highest praise for a basketball — is the most pathetically inadequate compliment possible.
jokes.explanation: 'It still works and everything' as praise for a basketball — the bar he's setting is 'it hasn't deflated in years' — is a wonderful piece of teen male social incompetence. Daniel is trying hard and failing completely. The 'and everything' is the detail that seals it.

jokes.text: Daniel asks if he can take the remaining Fruit Roll-Ups. Harold: 'Please. Take them.' Daniel takes them. 'Thanks so much for dinner and ice cream and everything. It was really good to meet you guys. Super nice.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is trying to leave on good terms after causing chaos.
jokes.punchline: 'Super nice' — Daniel's final social assessment of the Weirs — is the verbal equivalent of a D student's essay, doing the minimum possible work.
jokes.explanation: 'Super nice' as the closing of Daniel's social performance is funny precisely because it's so thin. After everything that happened, his parting judgment is 'super nice.' Works as character comedy.

jokes.text: Nick: 'Thanks so much for dinner and ice cream and everything. It was really good to meet you guys. Super nice.' / Then asks to leave his car at their house overnight.
jokes.setup: Nick is wrapping up the visit with pleasantries
jokes.punchline: After the warmest possible send-off, he asks to leave his car at their house — which means he'll need to return, extending the imposition
jokes.explanation: The escalating ask after the warm farewell is a classic Nick move — he's so endearing that people keep agreeing to things. Jean's 'Maybe we can all have dinner tomorrow night' response, inviting even more of this chaos, is the real punchline: she's been completely charmed despite everything.

jokes.text: Sam, desperately: 'Well, I got a ton of homework to do, so I guess I better go hit the books. Huh, Dad?' / Then just: 'Good night.'
jokes.setup: Jean is suggesting everyone have dinner again tomorrow; Sam desperately wants these people gone
jokes.punchline: Sam tries to get his parents to end the evening by fake-invoking homework — seeking paternal backup with 'Huh, Dad?' — and when that doesn't land, just quietly retreats with 'Good night'
jokes.explanation: Sam's failed escape attempt is a good little beat. The 'Huh, Dad?' seeking backup is very adolescent. His giving up and just saying 'Good night' and leaving is the right comedic deflation.

jokes.text: Harold and Jean's final exchange: 'This isn't good.' 'No.'
jokes.setup: The evening of chaos has concluded; Kim has just invited herself to dinner tomorrow.
jokes.punchline: Two words each: 'This isn't good.' 'No.' — the most economical possible parental assessment of the situation they've just lived through.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best deadpan closing line. Harold and Jean agree in four words that the night has been a disaster. The understatement of 'This isn't good' after everything — the screaming, the basketball evaluation, the worms, the Fruit Roll-Ups, 'Congratulations,' the car — is hilarious. Perfect economy, perfect character voice. Justifies the high economy score and earns 8+ overall.

jokes.text: Karen Scarfoli appears and demands Sam's essay: 'Hey, Sam Weir. Where's my essay?' — treating the principal's punishment as if it were a personal assignment she commissioned.
jokes.setup: Sam walks to class, still dealing with yesterday's humiliation.
jokes.punchline: Karen has reframed the vandalism punishment — the essay the principal assigned Sam — as an essay Sam owes HER, as if she's in charge of his consequences.
jokes.explanation: Karen demanding Sam's essay as if it belongs to her is brilliant character comedy — she has completely recontextualized his punishment as her entitlement. It reveals a worldview where Sam exists to fulfill obligations to her. The callback resolves the essay subplot with maximum Karen energy.

jokes.text: Vice principal posts Sam's essay on his locker so 'everyone at McKinley can learn from your little mistake.'
jokes.setup: Sam has written and turned in his vandalism essay.
jokes.punchline: The vice principal's 'solution' — posting the essay publicly, adding another layer of public humiliation to the kid who was already the victim — doubles down on institutional injustice.
jokes.explanation: The principal's total misreading of the situation — using Sam's public essay as an educational tool, making it worse — is consistent with the show's treatment of institutional authority. The callback resolves the subplot if not satisfyingly for Sam.

jokes.text: Vice principal posts Sam's essay on his locker: 'We're going to post this on your locker so that everyone in McKinley can learn from your little mistake.'
jokes.setup: Sam wrote the required essay; the VP now publicizes his humiliation
jokes.punchline: The essay — written to punish Sam for someone else's graffiti — is now posted on Sam's locker, which is itself another locker event
jokes.explanation: The locker continues to be the site of Sam's humiliation — now via officially sanctioned public shaming rather than graffiti. The irony of the institution punishing the victim is sustained through to the end.

jokes.text: Vice principal, surveying the kids: 'All right, I give up. If you kids want to live in trash, that's fine by me.'
jokes.setup: The vice principal has been trying to enforce decorum around the lockers.
jokes.punchline: His sudden complete capitulation — 'I give up' — and resignation to entropy is a funny beat from an authority figure.
jokes.explanation: A functional authority-figure joke. The 'I give up' is mildly funny but not distinctive.

jokes.text: Karen confronts whoever wrote on her locker: 'I did it. Because you are a slut, slut.' Other person: 'Hey, he hit on me.' Karen: 'Oh, yeah? Well, after school, I'm going to hit on you. See you then.'
jokes.setup: Karen confronts someone who apparently wrote on her locker in retaliation.
jokes.punchline: Karen repurposes 'hit on' — flipping from the sexual meaning to physical assault — as a threat, delivered with complete confidence and a casual 'see you then.'
jokes.explanation: 'Well, after school, I'm going to hit on you. See you then.' is a great line — the double meaning of 'hit on' is clean, the threat is specific (after school), and 'see you then' is delivered like a friendly appointment confirmation. Karen's casual menace is perfectly deployed. The pun structure is tight and the economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Kim, after Kim helped remove the locker graffiti for Sam: 'No problem, geek.'
jokes.setup: Sam thanks Kim for helping him with Karen.
jokes.punchline: 'No problem, geek.' — Kim accepts Sam's thanks but can't resist the diminutive, calling him 'geek' in a way that's now almost affectionate rather than cruel.
jokes.explanation: The episode's closing joke lands perfectly. 'No problem, geek' is a three-word callback that resolves the entire locker arc: 'geek' has been Sam's enemy-label all episode, and now Kim deploys it as something between a nickname and a term of rough affection. The context transform is what makes it work — the same word, completely changed by relationship. Perfect economy, perfect character integration, earns its score through narrative position and tonal precision. Justifies 8+.

tmdb_overview: Lindsay offers to help Daniel study for an algebra test, but ends up accused by the teacher of cheating. Daniel lends sexually naive Sam a porn film.
